trajectory.description: Observation: 2-epoch CLIP fine-tuning completed but val macro-F1 is essentially zero (0.0034), indicating the current setup is not learning useful class separation. The immediate failure was only in the local predict smoke test because /test_data/test.txt was unavailable; training/checkpointing worked. Analysis: full CLIP end-to-end fine-tuning at this scale is too slow (~1.7h for 2 epochs) and the current objective is poor for long-tail OOD generalization. A much stronger and faster baseline is frozen CLIP feature extraction plus a balanced linear classifier trained on all train features, with optional test-time ensembling over center + flip. This should drastically reduce iteration time and improve macro-F1 on rare classes via class-balanced loss/weights. Also predict.py should support fallback to val.txt for local smoke tests. Decision: rewrite model/train/predict around frozen CLIP embeddings + linear head, train the head for a few epochs, validate locally on val split, and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: the frozen-CLIP feature pipeline failed immediately because train.py wrapped the model in DataParallel and then called model.encode_image, which is not exposed on DataParallel. Analysis: this is a simple implementation bug, not a modeling failure. Given the previous end-to-end CLIP run was far too slow and poor, the frozen-feature approach is still the right direction because it should cut iteration time dramatically. We should fix DataParallel access, finish the feature-extraction + linear-head training pipeline, add a local val-format smoke test for predict.py, and submit eval right away to get a new baseline and timing. Decision: patch train.py to use a helper that accesses module.encode_image under DataParallel, keep frozen CLIP with balanced class weights, train for a few epochs, run predict.py on val split locally, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: frozen CLIP feature extraction plus linear head now trains successfully and reaches val macro-F1 0.2675 in about 6 minutes, a huge improvement over the earlier end-to-end baseline. The current failure is only in predict.py checkpoint loading: train saved a Sequential head state_dict with keys 1.weight/1.bias while predict expects a bare Linear with weight/bias. Analysis: this is a serialization mismatch, not a modeling issue. Since the baseline is already decent and fast, the highest-value next step is to make checkpoint format robust in both train and predict, run a local smoke test on val-format data, and submit eval immediately to get an official score/timing. Decision: patch train.py to save a consistent classifier/head state and patch predict.py to accept either bare Linear or Sequential-form checkpoints for backward compatibility, then run a short no-retrain pass if possible using the existing checkpoint and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: current frozen CLIP-B + linear head pipeline is working end-to-end, local prediction works, and official eval is 0.2339 macro-F1. Training is now fast enough (~6 min for feature extraction + head fit), so we have room to improve. Analysis: the main weakness is likely underfitting/domain shift from a single global linear head on center-cropped features. A strong low-cost upgrade is to use CLIP ViT-L/14 frozen features, multi-view feature extraction (center + flip) for train/val, and a stronger regularized MLP head trained on cached features with class-balanced loss. This should improve representation quality without the huge cost of end-to-end finetuning. Decision: patch model/train/predict to support cached frozen CLIP features, optional ViT-L backbone, 2-view feature averaging, and an MLP classifier with consistent checkpointing; train a short baseline with ViT-L and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.command: python train.py --model_name openai/clip-vit-large-patch14 --epochs 16 --batch_size 96 --head_batch_size 2048 --lr 0.002 --weight_decay 0.0005 --dropout 0.3 --hidden_dim 1024 --label_smoothing 0.05 --use_flip --domain_adv_weight 0.15 && python predict.py --data_path /data --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output val_preds.json
trajectory.description: Observation: frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 with 2-view averaged features and an MLP head improved official macro-F1 from 0.2339 to 0.2489, with val macro-F1 around 0.291. Analysis: representation quality is helping, but the plain supervised head is still likely overfitting common classes and location-specific shortcuts. We have enough time for one stronger iteration. A high-value next step is domain generalization at the feature level: use train location labels to learn a classifier that is invariant across locations by adding a small domain-adversarial head with gradient reversal on frozen CLIP features, while keeping class-balanced loss. This is cheap because only heads train on cached features. Also add logit ensembling between original and horizontally flipped views at inference if not already stored consistently. Decision: patch model/train/predict to support a feature-level DANN-style adversarial location head on cached CLIP features, train it on ViT-L features, validate locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: the DANN iteration failed late in validation due to a device mismatch inside cross-entropy: logits/labels were on CPU while the criterion's class weights were on CUDA. Analysis: this is an implementation bug, not evidence against the method. We should fix evaluation to move tensors to the criterion/device consistently, and also make training more robust by using separate weighted/unweighted criteria for class and domain losses. Since feature extraction is the expensive part, preserving the current approach and rerunning is the highest-value next step. Decision: patch train.py to fix device handling in evaluate_head and DANN training/eval, keep the ViT-L frozen-feature + DG head setup, run a moderate training job, smoke-test predict.py on val, and submit eval immediately if successful.

trajectory.description: Observation: the fixed ViT-L frozen-feature + domain-adversarial head produced a major jump to 0.3894 macro-F1, clearly the best result so far, but training curves show rising train accuracy with flat/noisy val accuracy and high val loss, suggesting overfitting/calibration issues. Analysis: with only ~3 hours left, the safest high-upside move is not a radical architecture change but a stronger inference/training regularization pass on the same successful pipeline. The most promising cheap gain is multi-view test-time ensembling beyond horizontal flip, plus label smoothing and EMA-like checkpoint selection via best val macro-F1. Since features are frozen, adding 5-crop style deterministic views is feasible at prediction time and can improve OOD robustness. To keep iteration risk low, I will preserve the current DG setup, add configurable multi-view feature extraction (center + flip + resized variants), add label smoothing for class loss, and train a moderate run. Then I will smoke-test predict.py locally and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: the latest modification catastrophically regressed official macro-F1 from 0.3894 to 0.1938, while the prior DANN-style ViT-L frozen-feature checkpoint is our clear best. Analysis: with only ~1.9 hours left, the highest expected value is to avoid further risky retraining and instead resubmit the known best checkpoint after verifying predict.py compatibility. The current workspace still has checkpoints/best.pt, but it was overwritten by the bad run, so we need to restore the last good configuration by reverting train/predict/model to the simpler successful DANN + flip setup and retraining once with the previously successful hyperparameters. This should fit in the remaining budget because the successful run took about 67 minutes. Decision: rewrite files to the last known-good stable version (no extra multi-view/label smoothing changes), retrain that exact setup, smoke-test on val, and submit eval.

trajectory.command: sed -n '1,260p' train.py && printf '\n==== METRICS ====\n' && tail -n 20 logs/iter_10/metrics.jsonl
trajectory.description: Observation: the attempted restoration failed badly again (official macro-F1 0.0679), while iteration 8 remains the clear best at 0.3894. Analysis: with only 0.827 hours left, retraining another full ViT-L run is risky and unlikely to beat simply recovering the known-good checkpoint. The workspace checkpoint was overwritten, but the framework history includes the exact successful stdout and indicates the code at iter 8 produced a valid end-to-end model. The safest move is to inspect current files/logs for clues, then restore the last-known-good simpler DANN configuration and train a shorter but faithful rerun, using the remaining time efficiently. If the local val macro-F1 is not close to the prior strong run, we should stop and avoid wasting time. Decision: inspect current train.py and metrics to identify the regression source quickly before deciding whether a short recovery retrain is feasible.
